1: wayyōsep̄ ʾĕlîhûʾ wayyōʾmar: 'Elihu continued and said.' Yāsap̄ (to add, continue) — Elihu resumes without being challenged or invited. His four-speech sequence (chs. 32-37) is structurally distinct from the three-friend dialogues: Elihu is never condemned by YHWH (42:7 explicitly names only Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar), and his speeches transition into the divine speech from the whirlwind. Whether Elihu's theology is vindicated, silently ignored, or simply incomplete is one of the book's interpretive cruxes.
2: kattēr-lî zeʿēr waʾahawwêḵā kî ʿôḏ lᵉʾĕlôah millîm: 'Bear with me a little longer and I will show you, for I still have words to speak for God.' Kattēr (wait a little, endure a moment — possibly from a root meaning 'wait') is a hapax. Millîm lᵉʾĕlôah: 'words for God' — Elihu positions himself as God's defense counsel. The claim to speak on God's behalf is significant: it's the same role Eliphaz, Bildad, and Zophar claimed, but YHWH eventually rejects their speech as wrong (42:7). Elihu's claim is not refuted, but neither is it explicitly affirmed.
3: ʾeśśāʾ ḏaʿtî lᵉmērāḥôq ûlᵉpōʿălî ʾetēn-ṣeḏeq: 'I will draw my knowledge from a wide range and ascribe righteousness to my Maker.' Lᵉmērāḥôq (from a distance, from afar) — Elihu draws on broad knowledge, not just immediate experience. The phrase anticipates YHWH's own speech in ch. 38-41, where God surveys cosmic and natural phenomena from the widest possible vantage point. Ṣeḏeq (righteousness, justice) — Elihu's purpose is theodicy: to vindicate God's ways.
4: kî-ʾāmnām lōʾ-šeqer millāy ûtᵉmîm ḏēʿôt ʿimmāḵ: 'For truly my words are not false; one complete in knowledge stands before you.' Elihu's self-description as tᵉmîm ḏēʿôt (complete/perfect in knowledge) is striking — the same adjective used of Job in 1:1 (tām = blameless, complete). Whether this is overconfidence or genuine claim depends on how YHWH's subsequent silence about Elihu is read. The claim of complete knowledge is exactly what the divine speech will implicitly deny to all humans — including Elihu.
5: hēn-ʾēl kabbîr wᵉlōʾ yimʾās kabbîr kōaḥ lēḇ: 'Behold, God is mighty and despises no one; he is mighty in strength of understanding.' Kabbîr (mighty, powerful — used of God's arm in Isa 17:12 and his voice in Job 37:23) occurs twice, framing God's power as both cosmic and relational. The key claim: God's might does not produce contempt (mā ʾas = to reject, despise) — an implicit rebuttal of Job's charge that God treats him with sovereign disregard. Kōaḥ lēḇ: 'strength of heart/mind' — divine power joined with divine intelligence and compassion.
6: lōʾ yeḥayyeh rāšāʿ ûmišpāṭ ʿăniyyîm yittēn: 'He does not keep the wicked alive, but gives justice to the poor.' The retribution principle stated positively: God's power works against the wicked and for the vulnerable. ʿĀnî (poor, humble, afflicted) — the classic prophetic concern for the oppressed (Amos 2:7; 5:12; Isa 61:1). Elihu is presenting a theodicy that takes the poor's justice seriously while maintaining divine sovereignty. The claim here is essentially the same as Eliphaz's (22:6-9), but Elihu arrives at it differently.
7: lōʾ-yiḡraʿ miṣṣaddîq ʿênāyw wᵉʾet-mᵉlāḵîm lakkisseʾ wayyôšiḇēm lānetsaḥ wayyiḡbāhû: 'He does not take his eyes off the righteous; he seats them on the throne with kings and exalts them forever.' Gāraʿ (to diminish, take away) — God does not diminish his gaze from the righteous. The elevated-to-king imagery (kissēʾ = throne) has a messianic resonance: the vindicated righteous person is ultimately seated on a royal throne. The language anticipates Job's own eventual restoration (42:12-16) and the larger biblical pattern of the humiliated righteous exalted (Ps 113:7-8; 1 Sam 2:8; Luke 1:52).
8: wᵉʾim ʾăsûrîm bazziqîm yillāḵᵉdûn bᵉḥeḇlê ʿōnî: 'And if they are bound in chains, caught in ropes of affliction.' The condition: if the righteous suffer (ʾăsûrîm bazziqîm = bound in chains, a prisoner's vocabulary). Ziqqîm (chains, fetters — rare; cf. Ps 149:8; Nah 3:10) and ḥeḇlê ʿōnî (cords of affliction) describe physical and experiential suffering as a condition that may befall even the righteous. This is Elihu's key distinction from the three friends: for Elihu, suffering can be pedagogical rather than purely punitive — the question is how the sufferer responds to it.
9: wayyaggēḏ lāhem pāʿolām ûp̄išʿêhem kî yiṯgabbārû: 'Then he shows them what they have done, their transgressions — that they have acted arrogantly.' Gābar (to be strong, to act arrogantly, to prevail) — the arrogance (yiṯgabbārû, hithpael = to make oneself great, to act presumptuously) is what suffering reveals and addresses. God uses affliction as a diagnostic revelation: the suffering exposes the self-sufficiency and pride that the sufferer may not otherwise acknowledge. This is Elihu's theodicy: God speaks through suffering to bring the proud to clarity about their actual condition.
10: wayyiḡl ʾāznam lammûsār wayyōʾmar kî-yāšuḇû mēʾāwen: 'He opens their ears to correction and commands that they turn from evil.' Gālāh ʾōzen (to open/uncover the ear) is the technical idiom for intimate disclosure — used of prophetic revelation (1 Sam 9:15; 2 Sam 7:27; Isa 22:14) and here for the insight that suffering produces. Mûsār (correction, discipline, instruction — the central Proverbs term) is what the suffering conveys. The ear-opening through suffering is exactly what Job needs: not to hear more arguments from the friends, but to receive divine revelation that opens his understanding.
11: ʾim-yišmᵉʿû wayyaʿōḇōḏû yᵉḵallû yᵉmêhem baṭṭôḇ ûšᵉnôṯêhem bannᵉʿîmîm: 'If they listen and serve him, they will complete their days in prosperity and their years in pleasantness.' The conditional blessing: šāmaʿ (hear/obey — the covenantal Shema verb) + ʿāḇad (to serve) leads to ṭôḇ (goodness, prosperity) and nᵉʿîm (pleasantness, delight — cf. Ps 16:11; 133:1). This is the Deuteronomic covenant blessing structure (Deut 28:1-14) applied individually. The promise is not false; the problem the book has demonstrated is that it cannot be reversed: prosperity does not prove obedience, nor does suffering prove sin.
12: wᵉʾim-lōʾ yišmᵉʿû bašelaḥ yaʿāḇōrû ûyiḡwᵉʿû bilᵉʾî-ḏāʿat: 'But if they do not listen, they will perish by the sword and die without ever understanding.' Bilᵉʾî ḏāʿat (without knowledge) — the worst outcome is not suffering but dying without ever understanding what the suffering was meant to teach. This death-without-insight is the opposite of the wisdom-goal that Proverbs sets: to die in full knowledge of God's ways. Elihu frames the stakes of Job's response: will he receive the instruction, or die incomprehendingly?
13: wᵉḥanpê lēḇ yāśîmû ʾāp̄ lōʾ-yᵉšawwᵉʿû kî ʾăsārām: 'The godless in heart store up anger; they do not cry to God when he binds them.' Ḥānēp̄ (godless, profane, polluted — used of one who has defiled the covenant, Isa 33:14; Jer 3:9) describes not atheism but practical denial of God's authority. Yāśîm ʾāp̄ (to store up/place anger) — resentment accumulating rather than being resolved in prayer. The contrast: the godless respond to divine binding with stored anger rather than with the prayer that would open them to instruction (lōʾ yᵉšawwᵉʿû = they do not cry out for help).
14: tāmōt bannōʿar nap̄šām wᵉḥayyāṯām baqqᵉḏēšîm: 'They die in youth, and their life ends among the cult prostitutes.' Qāḏēš (male cult prostitute, one devoted to a deity's sexual rites) — a reference to the Canaanite fertility cult practices condemned throughout the OT (Deut 23:17-18; 1 Kgs 14:24; 15:12; 22:46). Dying in youth and dying in the company of the cult prostitute are both signs of the dishonorable end: cut short, in a defiled context. The verse provides an ANE social illustration of how the path of refusing divine instruction terminates.
15: yaḥallēṣ ʿānî bᵉʿōnyô wᵉyiḡal balaḥaṣ ʾāznām: 'But he rescues the afflicted person through their suffering and opens their ears through oppression.' The paradox that is Elihu's central contribution: the rescue comes through the suffering (bᵉʿōnyô), not from outside it. Yiḡal (to open wide, uncover — from gālāh) through laḥaṣ (oppression, pressure) — the very pressure that the afflicted experience is the instrument of revelation. The ear-opening through pressure is the inverse of the hardening of the heart through prosperity; Elihu's theodicy makes affliction productive rather than punitive.
16: wᵉʾap̄ hᵉsîtᵉḵā mippî ṣar raḥāḇ lōʾ-mûṣāq taḥtêhā ûnaḥat šulḥānᵉḵā mālaʾ dāšen: 'Indeed, he was drawing you from the jaws of distress into a broad place, free from pressure, where your table would be spread with rich food.' Elihu applies the general principle to Job directly. Raḥāḇ (broad, spacious — the opposite of ṣar = narrow, confined) is the image of liberty and salvation (Ps 4:1; 18:19; 31:8). The promise of a full table (šulḥān mālaʾ dāšen = table full of fat/abundance) uses the hospitality image of Ps 23:5. Elihu sees Job's restoration as already initiated — God was in the process of deliverance before Job became impatient.
17: wᵉdîn rāšāʿ mālaʾtā dîn ûmišpāṭ yitmōḵū: 'But now you are full of the judgment due the wicked; judgment and justice have seized you.' Elihu's diagnosis of Job's current condition: he is experiencing the dîn rāšāʿ (judgment of the wicked) — not because he is wicked, but because his response to suffering has become the response of the godless (v.13). His accumulation of resentment and his legal challenge to God have moved him into the category of the wrongfully responding sufferer. This is a more nuanced charge than the friends' direct accusation of sin.
18: kî-ḥēmāh pᵉn-yᵉsîtᵉḵā ḇᵉśāpeq wᵉraḇ-kōp̄er ʾal-yaṭṭeḵā: 'Beware that anger does not lead you into scoffing; do not let the greatness of the required payment turn you away.' Ḥēmāh (wrath, rage) — the hot anger Job has expressed toward God. Śāpeq (scoffing, striking the hand, mockery) — the action of contemptuous rejection. Kōp̄er (ransom, expiation price, cover-price) — a technical legal term for the payment that resolves a legal dispute. Elihu warns that Job's rage may lead him to reject the divinely offered resolution of his case through scoffing pride.
19: hᵉyaʿᵃrōḵ šûʿāṯᵉḵā lōʾ bᵉṣar wᵉḵōl maʿămaddê-kōaḥ: 'Will your wealth hold you in distress, or all the forces of your strength?' The rhetorical question challenges Job's reliance on former resources. Šûʿāh (cry for help, distress) and maʿămaddê kōaḥ (resources of strength, what one can lean on) — even if Job marshaled all his former wealth and social power, these cannot resolve his case before God. The question strips away the human resources that might substitute for divine resolution.
20: ʾal-tišʾap̄ hallāylāh laʿălôt ʿammîm taḥtām: 'Do not long for the night, when peoples vanish from where they stand.' Šāʾap̄ hallāylāh (to pant/long for the night) — Job has expressed a longing for darkness and death (3:3-10; 7:4; 10:18-22). Elihu warns against this: the night in which peoples 'go up' (ʿālāh) from their place — i.e., the darkness of death — is not the resolution Job should seek. The warning is that seeking escape through death forecloses the possibility of resolution through response to divine instruction.
21: hišāmer ʾal-tēpen ʾel-ʾāwen kî-ʿal-zeh bāḥartā mēʿōnî: 'Be careful not to turn toward evil, for this is what you have been choosing over enduring affliction.' Bāḥar (to choose, elect — the covenantal election verb) — Elihu frames Job's response pattern as a choice: he has been choosing ʾāwen (evil, iniquity, nothingness) over ʿōnî (affliction, poverty). The word ʿōnî here means not just pain but the productive suffering through which God teaches (v.15). Job has chosen to fight his suffering rather than receive its instruction.
22: hēn ʾēl yāśgîḇ bᵉkōḥô mî kāmōhû môreh: 'Behold, God is exalted in his power; who is a teacher like him?' Śāgaḇ (to be exalted, inaccessibly high) — God's transcendence is not mere distance but the height that makes him the supreme teacher. Môreh (teacher, one who instructs) — a title used of human teachers (Isa 30:20) and here of God himself. The question mî kāmōhû (who is like him?) is the standard doxological rhetorical question (Exod 15:11; Mic 7:18; Ps 35:10). Elihu transitions from theodicy argument to praise: the God who uses suffering is the incomparable teacher.
23: mî-p̄āqaḏ ʿālāyw darkô ûmî-ʾāmar pāʿaltā ʿawlāh: 'Who has prescribed his path for him? Who can say to him, “You have done wrong”?' Pāqaḏ ʿāl darkô (to appoint/prescribe his path for him) — the verb of divine appointment used for human oversight of God. The question anticipates YHWH's own challenge to Job in 38:2-3: who dares to question God? Elihu frames the human inability to audit God's actions — the transition to the whirlwind speech is logical: no human can prescribe God's path, and YHWH's questions will demonstrate precisely this.
24: zᵉḵōr kî-taśgîʾā p̄āʿolô ʾăšer šōrᵉrû ʾănāšîm: 'Remember to extol his work, which people have celebrated in song.' Śāgāʾ (to magnify, make great) — extolling God's works is the antidote to the resentment that leads to rejecting divine instruction (v.13). The appeal to what humanity has sung (šārar = to sing, here in piel participle) about God's works is an appeal to the worshipping tradition: the psalms, hymns, and songs of Israel that celebrate divine action in creation and history. Job should situate himself within this tradition rather than positioning himself against it.
25: kāl-ʾāḏām ḥāzû-ḇô ʾĕnôš yabbîṭ mērāḥôq: 'All people have seen it; everyone looks at it from a distance.' The universality of natural revelation: all humanity can observe God's works in creation (ḥāzāh = to see, gaze upon — also used for prophetic vision). The looking from a distance (mērāḥôq) does not mean ignorance but appropriate creaturely distance before the inaccessible Creator. No human can stand close enough to fully comprehend; all humanity is in the position of observer-from-afar relative to God's cosmic operations.
26: hēn-ʾēl śāggîʾ wᵉlōʾ nēḏāʿ mispar šānāyw wᵉlōʾ ḥēqer: 'Behold, God is great, beyond our knowing; the number of his years cannot be counted.' Śāggîʾ (great, surpassingly great — an Aramaic-influenced form) — God's greatness is not merely quantitative but qualitatively beyond human comprehension. The un-numberable years (mispar šānāyw wᵉlōʾ ḥēqer = his years cannot be searched out) point to God's eternity: he antedates the world he created. This inaccessibility to counting and investigation is the foundation for the rhetorical questions of ch. 38-41.
27: kî yiḡraʿ niṭpê-māyim yāzōqû māṭār lᵉʾēḏô: 'For he draws up drops of water; they distill as rain from the mist.' Elihu begins the meteorological section (vv.27-33) that leads into the whirlwind speech. Gāraʿ niṭpê māyim (he draws up/diminishes drops of water) — the water cycle: evaporation is God's work. Zāqaq (to refine, distill, filter) — the rain is presented as a purification process, water drawn from the depths and refined through the atmosphere. The science is observational and accurate; the theological claim is that this natural process is God's continuous activity.
28: ʾăšer-yizzᵉlû šᵉḥāqîm ṭāp̄û ʿălê ʾāḏām rāḇ: 'Which the clouds pour down and drop on people in abundance.' Šᵉḥāqîm (clouds, thin clouds, sky-dust) — the upper atmosphere as the medium through which rain is delivered. Rāḇ (abundance, in great quantity) — the rain as extravagant divine provision, not merely adequate moisture but abundance. The movement from cosmic water-drawing (v.27) to abundant rainfall on humanity (v.28) describes God's care: what he draws from the depths he pours out for human benefit.
29: ʾap̄ ʾim-yāḇîn miprᵉśê ʿāḇ taršûʾôt sukkātô: 'Can anyone understand the spreading of the clouds or the thunder from his canopy?' Miprᵉśê ʿāḇ (spreading of thick clouds) — the extension of storm clouds across the sky. Taršûʾôt sukkātô: 'the thunderings of his pavilion/canopy' — God's dwelling is a storm canopy from which thunder (taršûʾāh = crash, clatter — hapax) rolls. The question (ʾim-yāḇîn = can anyone understand?) introduces the epistemological challenge: human observation of weather cannot penetrate to the divine agency behind it.
30: hēn pāraś ʿālāyw ʾôrô wayyᵉšōreš rāʾšê hayyām: 'See, he scatters his lightning around him and covers the roots of the sea.' Pāraś ʾôrô (he spreads his light/lightning) — the lightning is God's self-illumination broadcast around him. Yᵉšōreš rāʾšê hayyām: 'covers the roots of the sea' — the deepest foundations of the ocean are covered by divine illumination. The lightning reaching to the sea's depths is a statement about the vertical extent of God's presence: from the storm above to the ocean floor below, he is present and active.
31: kî-bām yāḏîn ʿammîm yitten-ʾōḵel lᵉmakbîr: 'For by these he governs peoples and provides food in abundance.' Yāḏîn ʿammîm (he judges/governs peoples) — the same storm phenomena that demonstrate God's cosmic power are the instruments of his historical governance of nations. Yitten ʾōḵel lᵉmakbîr: 'he gives food in abundance' — rain (vv.27-28) becomes food provision (cf. Matt 5:45: he sends rain on the just and unjust). The storm is simultaneously judicial and nourishing — God's dual use of natural phenomena.
32: ʿal-kappāyim kissāh ʾôr wayyᵉṣaw ʿālêhā bᵉmaprîaʿ: 'He covers his hands with the lightning and commands it to strike its mark.' The anthropomorphic image: God holds lightning in his hands (kappāyim = palms) and then releases it with a command. Maprîaʿ (that which strikes, the one who intercedes/encounters — disputed; possibly from pāgaʿ = to strike or meet) — the lightning is not random but directed: it hits its appointed mark. The divine volition behind each lightning strike is the theological claim.
33: yaggîḏ ʿālāyw rēʿô miqqᵉneh ʾap̄ ʿal-ʿôleh: 'Its thunder announces his presence; the cattle also sense the approaching storm.' Rēʿô (his companion/friend — or 'his thunder' depending on the vowel pointing; most read this as 'thunder' from rāʿam) — the thunder is God's herald, announcing his approach. Miqqᵉneh ʾap̄ (the cattle also — from qānāh = livestock) — even animals perceive the approach of the storm before humans do. The animals' instinctive sensing of divine weather activity is a subtle claim: creation is more responsive to its Creator than the humans who argue about him.
